the-ai-marketing-revolution.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to analyze your current marketing strategy and identify where AI could have the biggest impact.
the-ai-marketing-revolution.placeholder: Analyze my marketing and find AI opportunities...
the-ai-marketing-revolution.hint: I run marketing for a DTC skincare brand. Current channels: Instagram (20K followers), email (5K list), Google Ads ($2K/mo). My biggest time sinks: writing product descriptions, creating social captions, and A/B testing email subject lines. Where would AI make the biggest impact? Rank by time saved and revenue potential.
the-ai-marketing-revolution.badExample: How can AI help my marketing?
the-ai-marketing-revolution.goodExample: Analyze my DTC skincare marketing: Instagram (20K), email (5K list), Google Ads ($2K/mo). Time sinks: product descriptions, social captions, email A/B testing. Where does AI have the biggest impact? Rank by: time saved, revenue potential, and ease of implementation.
the-ai-marketing-revolution.successMessage: Mapping AI to your SPECIFIC marketing stack is step one. Generic AI advice wastes money — targeted AI prints it.

the-ai-marketing-revolution.instruction: List 3 marketing tasks you do every week that feel repetitive. For each, write one sentence about how AI could help.
the-ai-marketing-revolution.hint: 1. Writing social media captions (5 per week) — AI could draft first versions in my brand voice, cutting my time from 2 hours to 30 minutes. 2. Monthly performance reports — AI could pull key metrics and write the narrative summary automatically. 3. Responding to common DMs — AI could draft templated responses for FAQs I answer 10+ times weekly.
the-ai-marketing-revolution.successMessage: You just identified your AI quick wins. Start with the most repetitive one — that's where the ROI lives.

social-media-content-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a week's worth of social media posts for a specific brand. Include brand voice, audience, and platform.
social-media-content-with-ai.placeholder: Create a week of social posts for...
social-media-content-with-ai.hint: Create 5 Instagram posts (Mon-Fri) for a boutique coffee roaster. Audience: millennials who care about sustainability. Brand voice: warm, slightly nerdy about coffee science. Each post: caption (under 150 words), 3 hashtag suggestions, and a hook for the first line. Monday = educational, Wednesday = behind-the-scenes, Friday = community engagement.
social-media-content-with-ai.goodExample: Create 5 Instagram posts (Mon-Fri) for a boutique coffee roaster. Audience: sustainability-minded millennials. Voice: warm, nerdy about coffee science. Each post: caption (under 150 words), 3 hashtags, strong hook first line. Theme each day: Mon=educational, Wed=behind-the-scenes, Fri=engagement question.
social-media-content-with-ai.successMessage: A week of content in one prompt. Notice how brand voice + content themes + format specs create consistent, on-brand output.

social-media-content-with-ai.instruction: This social caption is generic and forgettable: 'Check out our new product! It's amazing and you'll love it. Link in bio!' Rewrite it for a specific brand and audience.
social-media-content-with-ai.hint: We obsessed over this one for 6 months. Our new cold brew concentrate is brewed for 18 hours with single-origin Ethiopian beans — and it makes your morning routine 3 minutes faster. One bottle = 12 cups. Your move, Monday. (Link in bio for early access, first 50 get free shipping.)
social-media-content-with-ai.badExample: Check out our new product! It's amazing and you'll love it. Link in bio!
social-media-content-with-ai.goodExample: We obsessed over this for 6 months. Our new cold brew concentrate: 18-hour brew, single-origin Ethiopian beans, makes your morning 3 minutes faster. 1 bottle = 12 cups. Your move, Monday. (Link in bio — first 50 orders get free shipping.)
social-media-content-with-ai.successMessage: Specific details, personality, and a reason to care. That's the difference between scroll-past and stop-and-read.

social-media-content-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to repurpose one piece of content into 5 different formats for different platforms.
social-media-content-with-ai.placeholder: Take this [content] and turn it into...
social-media-content-with-ai.hint: I wrote a 1,500-word blog post about '5 Signs Your Marketing Strategy Needs AI.' Repurpose it into: 1) A Twitter/X thread (8 tweets max), 2) A LinkedIn post (under 300 words), 3) An Instagram carousel outline (6 slides), 4) A TikTok script (30 seconds), 5) An email newsletter blurb (100 words). Keep the core message consistent across all formats.
social-media-content-with-ai.goodExample: Repurpose my 1,500-word blog '5 Signs Your Marketing Needs AI' into: 1) X thread (8 tweets), 2) LinkedIn post (300 words), 3) Instagram carousel (6 slides with headline + key point each), 4) TikTok script (30 sec), 5) Newsletter blurb (100 words). Same core message, platform-native format.
social-media-content-with-ai.successMessage: One piece of content, five platforms. This is how top marketers 10x their output without 10x the work.

blog-content-and-seo.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create an SEO-optimized blog outline. Include your target keyword, audience, and content goals.
blog-content-and-seo.placeholder: Create an SEO blog outline for...
blog-content-and-seo.hint: Create a blog outline targeting the keyword 'best CRM for small business 2025.' Audience: non-technical small business owners comparing CRMs for the first time. Format: H1 title (with keyword), 6 H2 sections, 2-3 bullet points under each H2 describing what to cover. Include a FAQ section with 4 questions. The post should be comprehensive enough to rank but scannable.
blog-content-and-seo.badExample: Write a blog post about CRMs
blog-content-and-seo.goodExample: Create an SEO blog outline for 'best CRM for small business 2025.' Audience: non-technical first-time CRM buyers. Structure: H1 with keyword, 6 H2 sections, 2-3 bullets each describing coverage. Include FAQ (4 questions people actually search). Goal: rank on page 1, be scannable, drive demo signups.
blog-content-and-seo.successMessage: An SEO outline saves hours of writing and ensures you hit the right keywords naturally. Structure before prose — always.

blog-content-and-seo.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to generate meta titles and descriptions for a blog post that maximize click-through rate.
blog-content-and-seo.placeholder: Write meta titles and descriptions for...
blog-content-and-seo.hint: Generate 3 meta title options and 2 meta description options for a blog post about 'How to Start a Podcast in 2025.' Meta titles: under 60 characters, include the keyword, create curiosity or promise a benefit. Meta descriptions: under 155 characters, include a call to action, mention something specific (like 'step-by-step guide' or 'under $100').
blog-content-and-seo.goodExample: Generate 3 meta titles (under 60 chars) and 2 meta descriptions (under 155 chars) for 'How to Start a Podcast in 2025.' Titles: include keyword, create curiosity OR promise a result. Descriptions: include CTA, mention something specific like 'step-by-step' or cost. Optimize for CTR.
blog-content-and-seo.successMessage: Meta titles and descriptions are your first impression on Google. A few extra minutes here can double your click-through rate.

ad-copy-that-converts.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create ad copy for a specific product, platform, and audience. Include the offer and desired action.
ad-copy-that-converts.hint: Write 3 Facebook ad variations for a meal planning app targeting busy parents. Offer: 14-day free trial. Each ad: headline (under 40 chars), primary text (under 125 words), CTA button text. Variation 1: pain point focused (no time to cook). Variation 2: benefit focused (healthy meals, less stress). Variation 3: social proof (join 50K+ parents).
ad-copy-that-converts.badExample: Write an ad for my app
ad-copy-that-converts.goodExample: Write 3 Facebook ad variations for a meal planning app. Audience: busy parents, ages 28-42. Offer: 14-day free trial. Each ad: headline (40 chars), primary text (125 words), CTA. Angles: 1) pain point (no time to cook), 2) benefit (healthy + easy), 3) social proof (50K+ users).
ad-copy-that-converts.successMessage: Three angles for the same product lets you A/B test what resonates. Pain, benefit, and proof — the holy trinity of ad copy.

ad-copy-that-converts.instruction: This ad headline is weak: 'Great Product At A Great Price.' Rewrite it for a specific product with a specific value proposition.
ad-copy-that-converts.hint: Meal prep done. Grocery list sorted. Dinner stress? Gone. (Try PlanMyMeals free for 14 days.)
ad-copy-that-converts.badExample: Great Product At A Great Price
ad-copy-that-converts.goodExample: Dinner decided in 30 seconds. Groceries auto-listed. Stress deleted. Try PlanMyMeals free — 14 days, no credit card.
ad-copy-that-converts.successMessage: Specific beats generic every time. 'Dinner decided in 30 seconds' sells — 'great product' doesn't.

ai-powered-email-marketing.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers. Include your brand, product, and goal for each email.
ai-powered-email-marketing.hint: Create a 5-email welcome sequence for new subscribers to a personal finance blog. Brand voice: friendly, no judgment, practical. Goal: move subscribers from 'curious' to 'trusting' to 'purchasing our budgeting course ($49).' Email 1 (Day 0): Welcome + free resource. Email 2 (Day 2): Our story + credibility. Email 3 (Day 5): Best content piece. Email 4 (Day 8): Subscriber success story. Email 5 (Day 12): Soft pitch for course. Each email: subject line + 150-word body.
ai-powered-email-marketing.goodExample: Create a 5-email welcome sequence for a personal finance blog. Voice: friendly, no judgment. Goal: build trust → sell budgeting course ($49). Emails: Day 0 (welcome + freebie), Day 2 (our story), Day 5 (best article), Day 8 (success story), Day 12 (soft pitch). Each: subject line + 150-word body.
ai-powered-email-marketing.successMessage: A strategic email sequence with clear goals for each touchpoint. This is how you nurture instead of spam.

ai-powered-email-marketing.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to generate 10 email subject lines for a specific campaign. Include the goal, audience, and any constraints.
ai-powered-email-marketing.hint: Generate 10 email subject lines for a Black Friday sale (30% off all courses). Audience: existing customers who've bought at least one course. Constraints: under 50 characters, no ALL CAPS, no spam trigger words (free, act now, limited time), 5 should be curiosity-driven and 5 should be benefit-driven. Include emoji options for 3 of them.
ai-powered-email-marketing.badExample: Write subject lines for my sale
ai-powered-email-marketing.goodExample: Generate 10 subject lines for a Black Friday sale (30% off courses). Audience: existing customers. Under 50 chars. No ALL CAPS or spam words. 5 curiosity-driven, 5 benefit-driven. Include emoji options for 3. Optimize for open rate, not clickbait.
ai-powered-email-marketing.successMessage: Ten subject lines in seconds means you can A/B test the top 2. Data-driven email marketing starts with volume.

content-calendars-and-planning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a 2-week content calendar for a specific brand across multiple platforms.
content-calendars-and-planning.hint: Create a 2-week content calendar for a yoga studio. Platforms: Instagram (daily), Facebook (3x/week), email newsletter (weekly). Content themes: Mon=motivation, Wed=technique tips, Fri=community spotlight, Sun=class schedule. Include: date, platform, content type (reel/carousel/story/post), caption concept, and any calls to action. Format as a table.
content-calendars-and-planning.goodExample: Create a 2-week content calendar for a yoga studio. Instagram (daily), Facebook (3x/wk), email (weekly). Themes: Mon=motivation, Wed=tips, Fri=community, Sun=schedule. Table format: Date | Platform | Content Type (reel/carousel/story) | Caption Concept | CTA. Include 1 promotional post per week.
content-calendars-and-planning.successMessage: Two weeks of content, planned in minutes. A content calendar turns chaos into consistency.

content-calendars-and-planning.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to brainstorm 20 content ideas for a specific niche, organized by content pillar.
content-calendars-and-planning.hint: Brainstorm 20 content ideas for a sustainable fashion brand. Organize by 4 content pillars: Education (how sustainable fashion works), Behind-the-scenes (our process), Community (customer stories), Product (styling and new arrivals). 5 ideas per pillar. For each idea, note the best format: reel, carousel, blog, or email.
content-calendars-and-planning.goodExample: Brainstorm 20 content ideas for a sustainable fashion brand. 4 pillars, 5 ideas each: Education (how sustainability works), BTS (our process), Community (customer stories), Product (styling/launches). For each: best format (reel/carousel/blog/email) and one-line description.
content-calendars-and-planning.successMessage: Content pillars prevent the 'what do I post today?' panic. Twenty ideas in your bank means you're always ready.

landing-pages-and-conversion.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to write landing page copy for a specific product or service. Include the sections you need.
landing-pages-and-conversion.hint: Write landing page copy for an online course called 'Excel for Managers.' Target: mid-level managers who are embarrassed they can't use Excel well. Sections needed: Hero headline + subhead, 3 pain points, 3 benefits, social proof section (placeholder for testimonials), pricing ($97), FAQ (4 questions), and a final CTA. Tone: empathetic, not condescending.
landing-pages-and-conversion.goodExample: Write landing page copy for 'Excel for Managers' course ($97). Audience: mid-level managers embarrassed about weak Excel skills. Sections: hero (headline + subhead), 3 pain points, 3 benefits, social proof placeholder, pricing, FAQ (4 Qs), final CTA. Tone: empathetic, never condescending. Goal: course enrollment.
landing-pages-and-conversion.successMessage: Landing page copy with clear sections, audience empathy, and conversion goals. This is how you turn visitors into customers.

landing-pages-and-conversion.instruction: This CTA button is weak: 'Submit.' Rewrite it for 3 different contexts: a free trial, a newsletter signup, and a course purchase.
landing-pages-and-conversion.hint: 1. Free trial: 'Start My Free 14-Day Trial' 2. Newsletter: 'Send Me Weekly Marketing Tips' 3. Course: 'Enroll Now — Start Learning Today'
landing-pages-and-conversion.goodExample: 1. Free trial: 'Start My Free 14-Day Trial — No Card Required' 2. Newsletter: 'Yes, Send Me Weekly Marketing Tips' 3. Course: 'Enroll Now & Get Instant Access'
landing-pages-and-conversion.successMessage: A good CTA tells people what they GET, not what they DO. 'Start My Free Trial' beats 'Submit' by 30%+ in click rates.

competitive-analysis-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to perform a competitive analysis framework for your brand vs. 2-3 competitors.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.hint: Perform a competitive analysis of my online yoga class platform vs. Alo Moves and Glo. Analyze each on: pricing model, target audience, content variety, unique selling proposition, social media presence, and biggest weakness. Format as a comparison table. Then give me 3 opportunities where I can differentiate.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.goodExample: Competitive analysis: my online yoga platform vs. Alo Moves and Glo. Compare: pricing, target audience, content variety, USP, social presence, biggest weakness. Table format. Then identify 3 differentiation opportunities for my brand based on gaps in their offerings.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.successMessage: A structured competitive analysis reveals gaps in the market. Those gaps are your opportunities.

competitive-analysis-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to analyze a competitor's content strategy and identify what you can learn from it.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.hint: Analyze the content strategy of Mailchimp's blog and resource center. What topics do they cover? How often do they post? What formats do they use (guides, case studies, tutorials)? What keywords are they clearly targeting? Based on this analysis, give me 5 content gaps they're missing that my email marketing tool could fill.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.goodExample: Analyze Mailchimp's content strategy: topics, posting frequency, formats (guides/case studies/tutorials), target keywords. Then identify 5 content gaps they're missing that my email marketing tool blog could fill. For each gap: topic idea, keyword opportunity, and estimated search volume range.
competitive-analysis-with-ai.successMessage: Learning from competitors isn't copying — it's intelligence. Find what they miss, then own that space.

analytics-and-reporting.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a marketing report template that tells a story, not just lists numbers.
analytics-and-reporting.placeholder: Create a monthly marketing report that...
analytics-and-reporting.hint: Create a monthly marketing report template for a B2B SaaS company. Sections: Executive Summary (3 sentences), Channel Performance (table: channel, spend, leads, CPL, trend vs last month), Top 3 Wins, Top 3 Concerns, Next Month's Focus (3 priorities). Include guidance for how to write the narrative — not just numbers but what they MEAN and what we should DO about them.
analytics-and-reporting.goodExample: Create a monthly marketing report template for B2B SaaS. Sections: Executive Summary (3 sentences), Channel Performance (table: channel/spend/leads/CPL/trend), Top 3 Wins, Top 3 Concerns, Next Month's Priorities. Include narrative guidance: each metric needs a 'so what' — what does the number mean and what action does it suggest?
analytics-and-reporting.successMessage: Reports that tell stories get read. Reports that list numbers get ignored. The 'so what' after each metric is where the value lives.

analytics-and-reporting.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to explain a marketing metric drop and suggest possible causes and fixes.
analytics-and-reporting.placeholder: Our [metric] dropped by [amount]. Why and what do we do?
analytics-and-reporting.hint: Our email open rate dropped from 28% to 19% over the last 3 months. List size grew from 5K to 12K in that period (we ran a lead magnet campaign). Give me 5 possible causes ranked by likelihood, and for each cause, one specific action to test. Also: what's a healthy open rate benchmark for a B2B newsletter with 12K subscribers?
analytics-and-reporting.goodExample: Email open rate dropped from 28% to 19% over 3 months. List grew 5K→12K via lead magnet campaign. Give me 5 causes ranked by likelihood + one fix per cause. Include a healthy benchmark for B2B newsletters at 12K subscribers. Format: ranked list with cause, likelihood, and action.
analytics-and-reporting.successMessage: Context turns a panic ('our rates are dropping!') into a diagnosis. Real numbers + timeline = actionable analysis.
